narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67.4% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.70—which is actually stronger than the 3.24 average among students who passed the bar—and other factors like your LSAT score of 31, your Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles both at 2, your middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The three biggest influences on this outcome were your Year 3 grade decile, your Year 1 grade decile, and your undergraduate GPA. Your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the 5.91 average for bar passers, had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance might not sustain the demands of law school. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to the 6.04 average among those who passed, reinforced this concern by highlighting early academic challenges. On a brighter note, your solid undergraduate GPA pushed back against failure, acting as a positive factor since it exceeds what successful bar takers typically show. Compared to admitted students, who often have higher early and later grade deciles and stronger LSAT scores around 37, your profile aligns more closely with those predicted to struggle, even with your full-time commitment and middle-income background that matches a common group among passers. While your strengths like that GPA are encouraging, the model weighed the lower deciles most heavily in forecasting bar risks. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and if possible, strengthen your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program. You might also seek mentorship from law school alumni who succeeded with comparable starts, and reapply after addressing these key areas to shift your predicted odds. With persistence, you can rewrite this story.
